title: Somatic Therapy for Overwhelm: When Everything Is Too Much
excerpt: Discover how somatic therapy helps regulate your nervous system to manage overwhelm when life feels too intense.
body:
Understanding Overwhelm: Why Does Everything Feel Like Too Much?

Overwhelm isn’t just a state of mind — it’s a full-body experience. When your nervous system is flooded with stress, it can feel like your body and brain are stuck in a loop of hyperarousal or shutdown. This is where somatic therapy steps in, focusing on the body’s role in processing and releasing overwhelm.

The Nervous System and Overwhelm

According to Dr. Stephen Porges, the autonomic nervous system (ANS) regulates our responses to stress through three primary states: social engagement (safe), fight/flight (mobilized), and freeze/shutdown (immobilized). When overwhelm takes hold, it often means the ANS is stuck in fight/flight or freeze, making it hard to think clearly, make decisions, or feel grounded.

Why Talk Therapy Alone Often Isn’t Enough

Many people who seek therapy for overwhelm find it challenging to move beyond the stuck feelings despite cognitive work. Bessel van der Kolk, in his seminal book The Body Keeps the Score, emphasizes that trauma and overwhelm are stored in the body. Talking alone can miss the somatic imprint of stress — the muscle tension, breath patterns, and nervous system dysregulation that keep us trapped.

Embodiment and Integration

Somatic therapy bridges this gap by bringing awareness to bodily sensations and using movement, breath, and touch to renegotiate the nervous system’s response. Pat Ogden’s Sensorimotor Psychotherapy and Peter Levine’s Somatic Experiencing are key methods that help clients track sensations and complete incomplete defensive responses, allowing the body to return to equilibrium.

"Overwhelm is not just psychological; it is a physiological state that somatic therapy can help regulate and resolve." — The Oracle Lover

How Somatic Therapy Helps With Overwhelm

Somatic therapy focuses on:

  • Nervous system regulation: Using techniques to downshift hyperarousal or gently mobilize shutdown.
  • Interoceptive awareness: Increasing sensitivity to internal body signals to recognize early stress cues.
  • Resourcing and grounding: Building internal and external supports to feel safe.
  • Processing incomplete responses: Helping the body complete defensive actions that were interrupted during stress or trauma.

Practical Tools in Somatic Therapy

Deb Dana’s work on Polyvagal Theory offers practical exercises such as breath regulation, safe touch, and social engagement cues that help shift the nervous system from a state of overwhelm to calm. These tools teach the nervous system new ways to respond to triggers and stressors.

Recognizing Your Body’s Signals of Overwhelm

It’s often easier to ignore or rationalize overwhelm, but tuning into your body can provide vital information. Common somatic signs of overwhelm include:

  • Tense muscles, especially in the neck, shoulders, or jaw
  • Shallow or rapid breathing
  • Digestive issues like nausea or butterflies
  • Heart racing or palpitations
  • Feeling frozen or disconnected from your body

Pat Ogden encourages a mindful, curious approach to these sensations, asking “What is this feeling trying to tell me?” rather than pushing it away.

Self-Directed Somatic Practices to Start Now

If you’ve tried talk therapy but still feel stuck, incorporating somatic tools can begin to change your nervous system’s default. Here are some evidence-supported practices:

  • Tracking sensations: Spend a few minutes each day naming sensations in your body without judgment. This builds interoceptive awareness (Ogden, 2015).
  • Breath regulation: Slow, deep diaphragmatic breathing activates the parasympathetic nervous system (Porges, 2011).
  • Grounding exercises: Feel your feet on the floor or your body in the chair to orient yourself to safety (Dana, 2018).
  • Movement: Gentle stretching or shaking can help discharge stored tension and complete defensive responses (Levine, 2010).

When to Seek Professional Somatic Therapy

While self-directed work can be powerful, some overwhelm is deeply rooted in trauma and requires skilled guidance. Somatic therapists trained in modalities like Somatic Experiencing, Sensorimotor Psychotherapy, or EMDR (Francine Shapiro) can help you safely access and process overwhelming sensations without retraumatization.

Gabor Maté highlights that trauma-informed somatic therapy addresses not just symptoms but the underlying survival strategies encoded in the body, creating lasting change.

Integrating Somatic Therapy With Other Modalities

Somatic therapy works best as part of a holistic approach. Integrating Internal Family Systems (Richard Schwartz) or mindfulness-based therapies with somatic work deepens the capacity for self-regulation and emotional resilience.

Dan Siegel’s concept of the "window of tolerance" reminds us that the goal is neither to suppress nor be overwhelmed by sensation, but to stay within a zone where the nervous system can adapt and learn.

Final Thoughts: Overwhelm is Manageable

Feeling like everything is too much is a red flag from your nervous system asking for attention and care. Somatic therapy offers a science-backed, body-centered pathway to move from reactive overwhelm to regulated resilience. It’s not about pushing harder but learning to listen, feel, and respond differently.

Remember: the body remembers, but it also has the capacity to heal when given the right tools and support.

faqs.a: Somatic therapy is a body-centered approach that helps individuals become aware of and release physical tension and trauma stored in the body, promoting nervous system regulation.

faqs.q: How does somatic therapy differ from traditional talk therapy?
faqs.a: Unlike talk therapy, somatic therapy focuses on bodily sensations and uses movement, breath, and touch to process trauma and overwhelm, addressing the physiological roots rather than just cognitive aspects.

faqs.q: Can I practice somatic therapy techniques on my own?
faqs.a: Yes. Many somatic tools like breathwork, grounding, and body scanning can be done independently, but working with a trained somatic therapist is recommended for deeper trauma.

faqs.q: How long does it take to feel relief from overwhelm using somatic therapy?
faqs.a: Relief varies per individual; some notice shifts quickly with consistent practice, while others benefit from ongoing therapy over weeks or months to retrain their nervous system.

affiliateProducts.title: The Body Keeps the Score by Bessel van der Kolk
affiliateProducts.description: A foundational book explaining how trauma is stored in the body and how somatic therapies can aid healing.
